name: Trolldom Nordic
description: Trolldom, in the Nordic tradition, represents a complex tapestry of folk magic, divination, and spiritual practices deeply intertwined with the natural world and the ancestral spirits. Rooted in pre-Christian beliefs and persisting alongside Christianity for centuries, Trolldom emphasizes a reciprocal relationship with the landvättar (land spirits), the dead, and the forces of nature. Practitioners, often referred to as 'wise ones' or 'cunning folk,' sought to influence events, heal the sick, protect against misfortune, and commune with the otherworld through rituals, charms, and knowledge of herbs and stones. Key aspects included seiðr (a form of Norse shamanism), galdr (chanting or spellcasting), and spådom (divination). Trolldom wasn't a monolithic system but rather a collection of localized traditions, varying in practice and emphasis. It emphasized practical application and personal experience over rigid dogma, often incorporating elements of animism and ancestor veneration. The impact of Trolldom on Scandinavian folklore and cultural identity remains significant, influencing contemporary pagan and magical practices.
subtitle: Nordic Folk Magic and Spirit Work

keyMyths.title: Odin's Acquisition of the Runes
keyMyths.description: Odin hung himself on Yggdrasil for nine days and nights, sacrificing himself to himself in order to gain knowledge of the runes, powerful symbols of magic and wisdom. This story highlights the importance of sacrifice and seeking knowledge in Trolldom.

keyMyths.title: The Creation of Mistletoe
keyMyths.description: The story of Balder's death, where he was only vulnerable to mistletoe, highlights the power of seemingly insignificant things and the unpredictable nature of fate. This emphasizes the importance of attention to detail and understanding hidden weaknesses in Trolldom.

sources.author: Johannes Johannesen
sources.description: A comprehensive overview of historical Trolldom practices in Norway.
sources.text: A comprehensive overview of historical Trolldom practices in Norway.

sources.author: Claude Lecouteux
sources.work: Witches, Werewolves, and Fairies: Shapeshifters and Astral Doubles in the Middle Ages
sources.description: Examines the broader European context of folk magic, including relevant Scandinavian traditions.
sources.text: Examines the broader European context of folk magic, including relevant Scandinavian traditions.
